title: SEGMENT 14 — Failure modes: index hopping, low diversity, GC bias, dephasing

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Failure modes: index hopping, low diversity, GC bias, dephasing
No technology is without failure modes, and Illumina's failure modes are well-characterized — which means they're predictable, which means you can design experiments to avoid them.
Let me walk you through the main ones, because if you ever run an Illumina experiment that doesn't quite work, you'll find yourself diagnosing one of these.
Index hopping.
When you pool multiple indexed samples on the same flow cell, you're trusting that the index sequence on each fragment correctly identifies its sample of origin.

But in some library prep methods, particularly the ones that rely on PCR amplification of the pooled library, indices can swap between samples — fragments from sample A can end up carrying the index of sample B during the bridge amplification step.
This is called index hopping or index switching, and at certain rates and in certain experimental designs it can be a serious problem.
For most applications the hopping rate is well under one percent and doesn't matter.

For sensitive applications — particularly single-cell sequencing, where you're trying to assign each read to a specific cell, or low-frequency variant detection where a hopping rate of 0.5 percent is enough to obscure real signal — it matters a lot.
The standard mitigation is dual indexing, with both indices having to match correctly for a read to be assigned to a sample.
Dual indexing reduces the hopping problem by orders of magnitude, because the chance of both indices hopping coordinately is much smaller than the chance of one hopping.
Modern Illumina protocols use dual indexing almost universally for this reason.
Low diversity.
Illumina's base-calling algorithms work best when the four bases are roughly equally distributed across the clusters being imaged at each cycle.
In a typical genomic library, that's automatically true — random DNA is roughly 50 percent A and T, 50 percent G and C, mixed across many fragments.

But in some applications — particularly amplicon sequencing of a single PCR product, or 16S microbiome sequencing of a specific gene — every cluster on the flow cell has the same sequence at the same position.
When the base caller looks at cycle 30, every cluster might be calling G.

That's a problem for the imaging optics: with all clusters dark (in two-channel chemistry) or all clusters one color (in four-channel chemistry), it's harder to focus the optics and harder to calibrate per-cluster brightness.

The standard mitigation is to add a small amount of unrelated DNA — called a PhiX spike-in, named after the bacteriophage genome that's used as the source — to the library, providing the base-call diversity the algorithm needs.
PhiX is a genome that just happens to have nicely balanced composition and has become the universal calibration substrate for Illumina runs.
A typical run includes 1 to 30 percent PhiX, depending on how diverse the main library is.
GC bias.
Some genomic regions are very GC-rich — sometimes 75 percent or more GC — and some are very AT-rich.
Both extremes amplify less efficiently than balanced regions during the PCR steps of library prep and the bridge amplification on the flow cell.
The result is uneven coverage across the genome: GC-rich regions get fewer reads, AT-rich regions get fewer reads, the well-behaved middle gets more reads than its share.
For variant calling, low-coverage regions are blind spots — you can't call a variant if you have no reads.
For copy-number analysis, GC bias creates systematic artifacts that can look like deletions and duplications where there are none.

The standard mitigations are PCR-free library prep (when you can afford it), specialized polymerases tuned for difficult templates, and bioinformatic correction algorithms that estimate the GC bias from the data and adjust coverage accordingly.
Dephasing.
This is the most fundamental error mode of Illumina sequencing, and it's worth understanding in detail.
Remember that each cluster contains about a thousand identical DNA molecules, and during each cycle the polymerase is supposed to add exactly one new base to each molecule.
In practice, this isn't perfect.
Some small fraction of the molecules don't incorporate a new base in a given cycle — maybe the polymerase missed, maybe the blocker wasn't fully cleaved from the previous cycle.
Some small fraction incorporate two bases — maybe the blocker on the new nucleotide was defective.
So after cycle one, the cluster is mostly synchronized — all molecules at position 1 — but a few are at position 0, and a few are at position 2.
After cycle two, the distribution spreads further.
After cycle 100, the cluster contains molecules at many different positions: most at position 100, some at 99, some at 98, some at 101, some at 102, and so on.
This phase drift is called dephasing, and it causes the cluster's signal to become a mixture of multiple bases rather than a clean single-base signal.
As dephasing grows over the cycles, the signal-to-noise ratio degrades, and quality scores drop.
By cycle 300, the signal is noticeably mushier than at cycle 1.
This is why Illumina reads can't be made arbitrarily long — past a few hundred cycles, dephasing overwhelms the signal.

Modern instruments and chemistries are designed to minimize dephasing — more efficient incorporation, better blocker chemistry, more complete cleavage — and base-calling algorithms include explicit dephasing models that try to deconvolve the mixed signal back to the dominant base.
But the fundamental limit is real.
If you ever look at a quality-score-versus-cycle plot from a long Illumina run, you'll see quality starting high at cycle 1 and gradually declining toward the end.
That decline is dephasing at work.
Cluster density problems.
Too few clusters on the flow cell — you waste capacity, your data is lower-volume than expected.
Too many clusters — adjacent clusters overlap, the base caller can't distinguish them, you lose data to "overclustering.
" Getting the cluster density right requires careful library quantification before loading.
Underloading and overclustering are the two most common reasons a sequencing run gives less data than expected.
Modern instruments are more forgiving than older ones, but cluster density is still something experienced operators pay attention to.
These are the main failure modes.

There are others — adapter dimers, where two adapter pieces ligate to each other without an insert in between and end up dominating the read population; chimeras, where two fragments accidentally join during library prep; barcode crosstalk with similar index sequences; cross-contamination between adjacent flow cell lanes — but they're relatively rare and well-managed by current protocols.
The Illumina platform is robust, but it's not magical.
Knowing the failure modes is the difference between troubleshooting a problem in an hour and being baffled for a week.
